eli3: Interest rate models describe how interest rates evolve randomly over time. Unlike stocks, interest rates are mean-reverting (they don't wander off to infinity; they pull back toward a long-run level) and have complex term structure dynamics (short rates and long rates don't move in lockstep). These properties require specialised models beyond simple GBM.

There are two main families: short-rate models (model the instantaneous short rate r_t; derive bond prices from it) and forward-rate models (model the entire yield curve directly). Short-rate models: Vasicek (mean-reverting Gaussian), CIR (mean-reverting, always positive), Hull-White (time-varying mean, fits today's yield curve exactly). Forward-rate models: Heath-Jarrow-Morton (HJM) framework models all forward rates simultaneously.

These models are essential for pricing interest rate derivatives: bond options, caps, floors, swaptions, callable bonds, MBS. They're used by fixed income desks at banks and asset managers to hedge interest rate risk, price structured products, and manage duration.

whenWhy.title: Vasicek model: mean-reverting short rate
whenWhy.body: dr_t = κ(θ - r_t) dt + σ dW_t. Parameters: κ = mean reversion speed, θ = long-run mean rate, σ = volatility. Solution: r_t = r_0 e^{-κt} + θ(1 - e^{-κt}) + σ ∫₀ᵗ e^{-κ(t-s)} dW_s. Distribution: r_t ~ N(r_0 e^{-κt} + θ(1-e^{-κt}), σ²(1-e^{-2κt})/(2κ)). Bond price: P(t,T) = A(t,T) exp(-B(t,T) r_t), where B(t,T) = (1-e^{-κ(T-t)})/κ, and A(t,T) is a deterministic function. This is an affine model — bond prices are exponential-affine in r_t. Zero-coupon yield: y(t,T) = -ln(P(t,T))/(T-t) = -A(t,T)/(T-t) + B(t,T)/(T-t) × r_t. Vasicek limitation: r_t can go negative (Gaussian). Vasicek advantage: analytical tractability (bond prices, European bond options have closed forms).

whenWhy.title: Hull-White model: calibrating to the current yield curve
whenWhy.body: Hull-White (1990): dr_t = [θ(t) - κr_t] dt + σ dW_t. θ(t) is a time-varying function chosen to exactly reproduce today's observed yield curve P*(0,T) for all T. Calibration: θ(t) = ∂f*(0,t)/∂t + κf*(0,t) + σ²(1-e^{-2κt})/(2κ), where f*(0,t) is today's observed instantaneous forward rate. The Hull-White model is an extension of Vasicek that fits today's term structure by construction. This market-consistent calibration is essential for derivatives pricing — a model that doesn't match observable bond prices would produce arbitrage-free but practically useless option prices. Hull-White is the industry standard for pricing caps, floors, swaptions, and callable bonds. European swaption prices: closed-form; American/Bermudan swaptions: use trinomial tree or Monte Carlo.

whenWhy.title: CIR model: guaranteed positive rates
whenWhy.body: Cox-Ingersoll-Ross (1985): dr_t = κ(θ - r_t) dt + σ√r_t dW_t. The σ√r_t diffusion ensures r_t stays positive when 2κθ > σ² (Feller condition). Distribution: non-central chi-squared (scaled). Bond prices: affine structure P(t,T) = A(t,T) exp(-B(t,T)r_t) with different A,B than Vasicek. CIR was preferred in the pre-2015 era when negative rates were impossible. Post-2015 (ECB, BOJ pushed rates below zero), Gaussian models (Vasicek, Hull-White) became more relevant. Extended CIR (calibrated to current yield curve like Hull-White) used for swaptions. Key limitation: the square-root diffusion makes the model harder to calibrate and less flexible than Gaussian models.

deepDiveIntro: Heath-Jarrow-Morton (HJM) framework (1992): models the evolution of the entire forward rate curve f(t,T) (instantaneous forward rate at time t for maturity T) directly. Under risk-neutral measure Q: df(t,T) = α(t,T) dt + σ(t,T) dW_t. No-arbitrage (HJM drift condition): α(t,T) = σ(t,T) ∫_t^T σ(t,s) ds (the drift is fully determined by the volatility structure). The bond price: P(t,T) = exp(-∫_t^T f(t,s) ds). HJM is a general framework; Vasicek, CIR, and Hull-White are special cases with specific volatility functions σ(t,T). The Libor Market Model (LMM/BGM) is an HJM model for discretely compounded forward rates — the market standard for interest rate derivatives since the late 1990s.

deepDive.heading: LIBOR Market Model (Brace-Gatarek-Musiela)
deepDive.body: The LMM models a set of forward LIBOR rates L_k(t) (the rate at time t for the period [T_k, T_{k+1}]). Under the T_{k+1}-forward measure: dL_k(t) = σ_k(t) L_k(t) dW_k^{T_{k+1}}(t). This is a log-normal model for each forward rate — Black's formula applies, giving Black's caplet formula: caplet price = (T_{k+1} - T_k) × B(0, T_{k+1}) [L_k(0) N(d₁) - K N(d₂)], where d₁,d₂ are the standard Black formula terms with σ_k as volatility. Under a single measure, forward rates are not log-normal simultaneously — requires drift corrections. Monte Carlo simulation of the full rate curve is standard. Advantages: (1) Directly calibrates to observed cap/floor prices (market implied Black vols); (2) Natural for pricing caps, floors, swaptions; (3) Can be extended to stochastic volatility (SABR-LMM). Disadvantage: high-dimensional simulation needed for long-dated products. [1]

deepDive.heading: Negative Rates and the Shifted Lognormal Model
deepDive.body: Post-2015, ECB and BOJ drove short rates below zero (ECB deposit rate reached -0.5%). Standard log-normal models (CIR, LMM) cannot handle negative rates — log of a negative number is undefined. Solutions: (1) Shifted lognormal model: model L_k(t) + s as log-normal (shift s = 1-3%, so rates can go as low as -s); (2) Normal SABR: instead of log-normal dynamics, use normal (Gaussian) dynamics for the forward rate; rates can be negative; implied vol is 'normal vol' (in bps, not %); (3) Hull-White (Gaussian): already handles negative rates naturally. Practical calibration: for EUR and JPY markets post-2015, practitioners shifted to normal vol convention and shifted-lognormal/Hull-White models. USD, GBP markets retained log-normal convention. This created a period of model fragmentation across currencies. As rates normalised post-2022, log-normal models regained dominance. [2]

deepDive.heading: Yield Curve Factor Models: PCA Decomposition
deepDive.body: The yield curve moves in non-random ways: empirical analysis shows 3 principal components explain >95% of yield curve variance. PC1: parallel shift (level) — all maturities move together; explains ~80% of variance. PC2: slope (twist) — short rates and long rates move in opposite directions; explains ~12%. PC3: curvature (butterfly) — medium-term rates move vs. short and long rates; explains ~5%. Risk management application: hedge bond portfolio against these 3 PCs rather than against every maturity independently (duration ladder). Two-factor models: model short rate r and long rate l separately; captures level + slope. Three-factor models (Litterman-Scheinkman 1991): add curvature. Factor models for risk: PCA on daily yield changes → 3 factor shocks → repriced portfolio in each scenario → factor VaR. This is the standard approach for interest rate risk management in fixed income portfolios. [2]

takeaways: Vasicek: dr = κ(θ-r)dt + σdW; mean-reverting Gaussian; bond prices are affine; can go negative. [1]
Hull-White: adds time-varying θ(t) to calibrate exactly to today's yield curve — industry standard for IR derivatives. [1]
CIR: dr = κ(θ-r)dt + σ√r dW; stays positive (Feller condition: 2κθ > σ²). [1]
HJM drift condition: forward rate drift = σ × ∫σ ds — no-arbitrage restricts drift from vol. [1]
3 PCs explain >95% of yield curve moves: level (~80%), slope (~12%), curvature (~5%). [2]

edgeCases.q: How do you choose between Hull-White and LMM for a specific product?
edgeCases.a: Hull-White: best for products with American/Bermudan exercise (callable bonds, Bermudan swaptions) where tree methods work well; also best when you need a fast, analytically tractable model calibrated to the current curve; simpler calibration (κ, σ). LMM: best for products with complex cash flow structures, correlation-dependent payoffs (spread options between different maturity rates), or when you need to calibrate to the full cap/floor volatility smile simultaneously. LMM requires Monte Carlo (no tree), is slower, but can calibrate to 50+ volatility quotes simultaneously. Hybrid products: some practitioners use Hull-White for swaptions and LMM for caps within the same risk system — this creates model inconsistency but is pragmatically manageable.

edgeCases.q: What is the SOFR transition's impact on interest rate models?
edgeCases.a: LIBOR was replaced by risk-free rates (RFRs) globally by end-2021: SOFR (USD), SONIA (GBP), ESTR (EUR). Key modelling differences: (1) SOFR is an overnight rate compounded in arrears over a period; LIBOR was a term rate set in advance. This changes the timing of cash flow determination; (2) SOFR compounded in arrears is known only at end of the period — products referencing SOFR need different pricing framework than LIBOR products; (3) LMM directly models term LIBOR rates; for SOFR, models need to work with compounded overnight rates or, where available, term SOFR rates; (4) Correlation structure differs: SOFR is more volatile than LIBOR (which had built-in expectation smoothing); volatility surfaces need re-estimation. The transition required significant model updates at all major financial institutions.

sources.label: Hull, J.C. & White, A. (1990) — Pricing Interest-Rate-Derivative Securities, RFS
Litterman, R. & Scheinkman, J. (1991) — Common Factors Affecting Bond Returns, JPM
